title: Sorbus trilobata
intro: Sorbus trilobata, also known as the whitebeam or mountain ash, is a deciduous shrub or small tree native to Europe and Western Asia, characterized by its trilobed leaves and white flowers. The health benefits of Sorbus trilobata include its anti-inflammatory, antiviral, and antimicrobial properties, which make it a potential treatment for various ailments, including respiratory issues and infections. The bioactive constituents of this plant include flavonoids, phenolic acids, and terpenoids, which contribute to its medicinal properties. The most common herbal preparations made with Sorbus trilobata include teas, infusions, and tinctures, which are often used to promote health and well-being in traditional medicine.
taxonomy: Sorbus trilobata is classified as a species of flowering plant in the family Rosaceae, commonly known as the rose family. This family includes over 2,000 species, many of which are well-known for their beautiful flowers and edible fruits. Within the family Rosaceae, Sorbus trilobata is included in the genus Sorbus, which is a group of deciduous trees and shrubs native to the Northern Hemisphere. The genus Sorbus is further divided into several sections, with Sorbus trilobata belonging to the section Sorbus section Micromeles. This classification reflects the plant's evolutionary relationships and characteristics, such as its compound leaves and white flowers.
uses: Sorbus trilobata, also known as the Tibetan mountain ash, has been used in traditional medicine for its various health benefits. The plant's bark, leaves, and roots are said to have anti-inflammatory and antimicrobial properties. It is used to treat fever, rheumatism, and gastrointestinal issues. Traditional practitioners also use it to lower blood pressure and aid in digestion. However, more research is needed to confirm its effectiveness for medicinal purposes.

preparations: Sorbus trilobata is a lesser-known species within the Sorbus genus, which has limited documentation on its herbal preparations.
